text.title: Service Implementation: Docstrings, Transactions, Domain Exceptions
text.summary: Google-style docstrings, transaction.atomic wrapping, update_fields discipline, and per-module domain exceptions.

rule.statement: Write Google-style docstrings on every service function listing the business rules as bullets, then Args, Returns, and Raises.
rule.rationale: Business rules in comments are visible to reviewers and LLM agents without reading the implementation. Raises section lets view code handle domain exceptions cleanly.

example.code:
def create_order(user: "User", amount: "Decimal") -> "Order": """Create order for user.
Business logic: - Validates user daily limits - Creates Transaction and Order records - Sends confirmation notification
Args: user: User placing the order. amount: Order amount in base currency.
Returns: Created Order instance.
Raises: LimitExceededError: If daily limit is exceeded. """ ...

rule.statement: Wrap multi-write paths in @transaction.atomic; always pass update_fields=[] on partial model saves.
rule.rationale: Without atomic, a crash between two writes leaves the DB in a partial state. Without update_fields, Django fires every pre_save/post_save signal and does a full UPDATE even for unrelated fields — expensive and bug-prone.

example.code:
from django.db import transaction
@transaction.atomic def transfer_item(from_user: "User", to_user: "User", item_id: str) -> "Item": from apps.inventory.models import Item item = Item.objects.select_for_update().get(id=item_id, user=from_user) item.user = to_user item.save(update_fields=["user", "updated_at"]) # not full save return item

rule.statement: Define a <Feature>Error exception per service module; raise domain errors — never DRF ValidationError or Http404 — inside service code.
rule.rationale: DRF exceptions in services couple business logic to HTTP. Views map domain errors to HTTP responses cleanly; Celery tasks can log them differently.

antipattern.description: Importing DRF's Response, serializers.ValidationError, or any Django HttpResponse into service modules.
antipattern.reason: Services become untestable without a full DRF request context and unreusable from non-HTTP entry points like Celery.
